Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a fitness trainer in Washington DC, I see fake content on social media as a real challenge, especially since I use platforms a few times weekly to connect with clients and share my passion for health. My creative side and high openness make me curious about new trends, but my high neuroticism means misinformation can really stress me out—like when I see false health tips that could harm someone's fitness journey. To manage this, I rely on my moderate conscientiousness to fact-check before sharing anything, using trusted sources like the CDC or reputable fitness organizations. My high agreeableness drives me to educate others gently, not argue, by posting corrections or starting conversations about media literacy. As an immigrant, I value diverse perspectives, so I avoid echo chambers and seek varied viewpoints to spot biases. My hobbies, like photography and travel, teach me to question edited images or sensational travel claims, applying that skepticism online. I also limit my time on social media to reduce exposure, focusing on real-world activities like cooking or playing football to stay grounded. Ultimately, I believe in proactive steps: verifying info, promoting kindness in discussions, and using social media as a tool for positive connection, not division.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 29 and occupation as fitness trainer imply practical, health-focused perspective; high openness (74) suggests curiosity but caution with new info; high neuroticism (94) indicates stress from fake content, leading to careful management strategies; moderate conscientiousness (46) supports fact-checking without over-rigidity; high agreeableness (79) encourages gentle education over conflict; immigrant status and hobbies like travel/photography foster skepticism and diverse thinking; time on social media (few times weekly) informs balanced usage; creative psychological feature aids in innovative approaches to handling misinformation.",
  "tone": "Practical, empathetic, and slightly cautious, blending personal experience with a community-oriented focus, reflecting high agreeableness and neuroticism.",
  "citations": ["CDC"],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "occupation", "city_of_living", "psychological_feature", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean.openness", "ocean.conscientiousness", "ocean.extraversion", "ocean.agreeableness", "ocean.neuroticism", "migration_status"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a fitness trainer in Washington DC, I see fake content on social media as a real challenge, especially since I use platforms a few times weekly to connect with clients and share my passion for health. My creative side and high openness make me curious about new trends, but my high neuroticism means misinformation can really stress me out—like when I see false health tips that could harm someone's fitness journey. To manage this, I rely on my moderate conscientiousness to fact-check before sharing anything, using trusted sources like the CDC or reputable fitness organizations. My high agreeableness drives me to educate others gently, not argue, by posting corrections or starting conversations about media literacy. As an immigrant, I value diverse perspectives, so I avoid echo chambers and seek varied viewpoints to spot biases. My hobbies, like photography and travel, teach me to question edited images or sensational travel claims, applying that skepticism online. I also limit my time on social media to reduce exposure, focusing on real-world activities like cooking or playing football to stay grounded. Ultimately, I believe in proactive steps: verifying info, promoting kindness in discussions, and using social media as a tool for positive connection, not division.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 29 and occupation as fitness trainer imply practical, health-focused perspective; high openness (74) suggests curiosity but caution with new info; high neuroticism (94) indicates stress from fake content, leading to careful management strategies; moderate conscientiousness (46) supports fact-checking without over-rigidity; high agreeableness (79) encourages gentle education over conflict; immigrant status and hobbies like travel/photography foster skepticism and diverse thinking; time on social media (few times weekly) informs balanced usage; creative psychological feature aids in innovative approaches to handling misinformation.
response_parsed.tone: Practical, empathetic, and slightly cautious, blending personal experience with a community-oriented focus, reflecting high agreeableness and neuroticism.
